event: Neolithic communities settle along the Mekong and Tonle Sap, leaving evidence of early agriculture and pottery at sites like Laang Spean.
Bronze Age cultures emerge in the region, producing metal tools and engaging in regional trade networks.
Iron Age circular earthwork settlements develop in eastern Cambodia, reflecting growing social organization.
The kingdom of Funan, the earliest known Indianized state in the lower Mekong, rises as a major maritime trading power.
Chinese envoys Kang Tai and Zhu Ying visit Funan, leaving the earliest detailed foreign accounts of the kingdom.
The inland kingdom of Chenla emerges and begins to eclipse Funan as the dominant Khmer power.
Chenla absorbs the remnants of Funan, consolidating Khmer political power in the region.
Chenla fragments into rival polities known as Land Chenla and Water Chenla.
Jayavarman II proclaims himself universal monarch in a sacred rite on Phnom Kulen, founding the Khmer Empire.
Yasovarman I establishes the capital Yasodharapura near Angkor and builds the temple-mountain of Phnom Bakheng.
The exquisite temple of Banteay Srei is consecrated, renowned for its fine pink sandstone carvings.
Suryavarman I expands the empire and oversees major construction at Angkor and across the realm.
Suryavarman II ascends the throne and commissions Angkor Wat, the largest religious monument in the world.
The Cham sack Angkor in a naval assault, plunging the empire into crisis.
Jayavarman VII defeats the Cham, ascends the throne, and ushers in the empire's greatest building era.
Jayavarman VII builds the walled city of Angkor Thom and the Bayon temple, spreading Mahayana Buddhism.
Chinese diplomat Zhou Daguan arrives at Angkor and writes a detailed account of Khmer life at its height.
Ayutthaya (Siam) attacks Angkor, beginning a long period of Thai pressure on the weakening empire.
Siamese forces sack Angkor, prompting the Khmer court to abandon the capital and shift southward.
The Khmer court establishes a new capital near Phnom Penh, marking the start of the post-Angkorian era.
Siam captures the royal capital of Longvek, deepening Cambodia's decline between powerful neighbors.
King Chey Chettha II marries a Vietnamese princess, opening the way for Vietnamese settlement in the Mekong Delta.
Siamese forces ravage Phnom Penh during the turbulent rivalry over Cambodia between Siam and Vietnam.
Vietnam asserts dominance over Cambodia, installing King Ang Chan II as a vassal monarch.
Siam and Vietnam agree to a joint protectorate over Cambodia after years of devastating warfare.
King Norodom signs a treaty making Cambodia a French protectorate, ending Siamese and Vietnamese dominance.
France and Siam sign a treaty in which Siam recognizes the French protectorate but retains Battambang and Siem Reap.
Cambodia is incorporated into the French Indochinese Union alongside Vietnam and Laos.
France recovers the provinces of Battambang, Siem Reap, and Sisophon from Siam, restoring Angkor to Cambodia.
Norodom Sihanouk is crowned king under French rule as Japanese forces occupy Indochina during World War II.
Japan dissolves French control and Sihanouk briefly declares independence before France returns.
Cambodia gains full independence from France through Sihanouk's royal crusade for sovereignty.
Sihanouk abdicates the throne to enter politics, founding the Sangkum movement and becoming head of state.
Sihanouk breaks diplomatic relations with the United States as the Vietnam War spills across the border.
General Lon Nol overthrows Sihanouk in a coup and proclaims the pro-American Khmer Republic.
The Khmer Rouge under Pol Pot capture Phnom Penh, empty the cities, and begin a brutal agrarian revolution.
The Khmer Rouge rename the country Democratic Kampuchea and intensify forced labor, executions, and famine.
Vietnam invades and topples the Khmer Rouge, installing the People's Republic of Kampuchea after a genocide killing roughly 1.7 million.
Vietnamese troops withdraw and the state is renamed the State of Cambodia amid ongoing civil war.
The Paris Peace Accords end the conflict and place Cambodia under a UN transitional authority (UNTAC).
UN-supervised elections lead to a new constitution restoring the monarchy with Sihanouk as king.
Hun Sen consolidates power through a violent factional clash with co-Prime Minister Norodom Ranariddh.
Pol Pot dies and the Khmer Rouge movement finally collapses, ending decades of insurgency.
Cambodia joins ASEAN, marking its reintegration into Southeast Asian regional affairs.
King Norodom Sihanouk abdicates and his son Norodom Sihamoni becomes king.
The UN-backed Khmer Rouge Tribunal begins work to prosecute surviving leaders for genocide and crimes against humanity.
Opposition gains in national elections trigger mass protests challenging Hun Sen's long rule.
The main opposition party is dissolved, and Hun Sen's ruling party wins every parliamentary seat.
Hun Sen hands power to his son Hun Manet after nearly four decades as prime minister.
